explicit_text: Jim Collins, who wrote Good to Great, really influenced how I think about leadership and team building. His idea about getting the right people on the bus at the right time and in the right seats resonates with me deeply. I strive to bring out the best in each team member, focusing on their strengths rather than trying to change their personalities or temperaments. His work helped shape my approach to leading medical missions in the military.

explicit_text: Serving in the United States military has taught me a lot about mission focus and leadership. In the military, the mission always comes first, and you have to figure out how to get it done with the people you have. Unlike combat commanders who may have to send people into harm’s way, my role in medical missions is to coordinate and bring out the best in my team to save lives. The military context shapes how we prepare and execute humanitarian assistance missions.

explicit_text: I participated in the Continuing Promise mission before being selected as skipper, which gave me valuable experience with the Comfort and how these ships are repurposed for humanitarian assistance. Continuing Promise 2010 was particularly important because it helped me understand the complexities of coordinating NGOs and medical operations on board. This experience proved crucial when we later responded to the Haiti earthquake disaster.

This experience proved crucial when we later responded to the Haiti earthquake disaster.

explicit_text: The United States Navy operates ships like the Comfort, which I commanded during humanitarian missions. These ships were originally built for Cold War purposes but have been adapted for medical and disaster relief roles. My role as a Navy captain involved pulling together diverse teams and coordinating with civilian agencies and NGOs to maximize our impact during missions like the Haiti earthquake response.

explicit_text: I was driving home to Annapolis, Maryland, when I first heard about the 2010 Haiti earthquake on the radio. That moment made me realize the American government would respond quickly, and I started thinking about how our ship and team could be involved. Annapolis is where I live, and it was the place where the initial call to action began for me.

explicit_text: The 2010 Haiti earthquake was a devastating 7.0 magnitude disaster that triggered a rapid response from the U.S. government and military. I first heard about it on the radio while driving home and immediately knew our medical ship might be called to assist. The scale of the disaster was initially underestimated, but as more information came in, we prepared to deploy the Comfort and coordinate with NGOs and USAID to provide relief.

explicit_text: The USNS Mercy had just completed an overhaul in San Francisco when the Haiti earthquake happened, so it was unavailable to deploy immediately. I had previously worked with people who served on the Mercy during humanitarian missions like Operation Smile. While the Mercy was out of pocket, we considered other Navy ships from Norfolk to respond, which ultimately led to the Comfort’s deployment.

explicit_text: Norfolk, Virginia, was a key location during the Haiti earthquake response as it was the home port for the Amphibious Ready Group that was spinning up to deploy. Tom Neegus, the commodore from our last Continuing Promise mission, was based there and contacted me early on about the possibility of going to Haiti. Norfolk served as a hub for military assets preparing for the relief operation.

explicit_text: USAID is the lead agency for health care in disaster relief, and I insisted on having their representatives on board the Comfort. Despite their usual reluctance to work directly with the military, I demanded their involvement to improve coordination and efficiency. USAID sent two key people, Dr. Clydette Powell and Rob Ferris, who played major roles in the Haiti mission.
